steps.title: Identify the passage's central argument
steps.content:
The passage describes Barragán's career evolving after he traveled abroad and absorbed functionalism and modernism. It then contrasts two buildings: the José Mojica apartment (unadorned geometric forms, modernist) and the Harper de Garibi House (historically inspired, an early project). The main idea is a stylistic shift driven by international exposure.
  • Choice A — 'Barragán's designs of the apartment building for José Mojica and the Harper de Garibi House are considered paragons of a functionalist and modernist aesthetic.': The Harper de Garibi House is historically inspired, not functionalist or modernist — this mischaracterizes both buildings. ✗
  • Choice B — 'A notable shift in Barragán's design aesthetic reflects the influence of his time abroad.': Directly captures the evolution (early historically inspired work $\Rightarrow$ later modernism) caused by international exposure. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Barragán's design of the Harper de Garibi House is considered more experimental than his design for the apartment building for José Mojica.': Reverses the relationship — the Mojica building, not the Harper house, embodies the newer, more experimental modernist style. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Barragán's early work shows an initial dedication to a modernist aesthetic that he later abandoned.': Reverses the timeline — his early work was historically inspired; modernism came later after his travels. ✗

steps.title: Match to the correct choice
steps.content: The passage's structure — travel abroad $\Rightarrow$ shift to modernism, contrasted against an earlier historically inspired project — points squarely to a stylistic evolution shaped by his international experience. That matches choice B — B is correct.
